All right, welcome back. We've got a great topic for this lesson after sales, meaning if you're in one of those industries, or even if you're not, but you might end up in one one day, right? Where you sell someone a product, but then after you close them, there are five or six other add-ons, other features, other ideas that can go along with what you're selling that together will make the product a better product, give it more value to the customer. And of course, in many cases, there's a lot of money in the after sales. Perfect example, this is in the automobile industry, where they have things like undercoating and rust-proofing and special types of rims for the wheels and whatnot, different types of exhaust for the engine, all these different things. And of course, on top of this also selling someone different financing options, right? Now, let me just say this. I don't want to just focus strictly on cars. And the reason is because I'm actually niche-ifying the straight line specifically for auto sales. So if you're in the auto sales world, guess what? In the very short term, you're going to have an awesome niche-fine straight line practice speaks directly to your industry if you don't already have it and are watching it right now. But I will say this. In the same vein that the automobile industry really runs on those after sales, a huge source of profit, there's also the same situation in the electronics industry. When you go to major stores, whether it's a Best Buy or a Harvey Norman in Australia or any one of these big franchises or even small mom and pops individual operators around the world, there's actually a lot more money made on the warranties they offer versus the actual product itself. And herein lies the rub. I'll tell you a story. I was shopping at a local store here. It's actually called Fry's Electronics in LA. And I blame it. I don't pay. You know all my stuff, right? On my head, low-actual. The one thing I love my vice TV sets. I love flat screen TVs. I will keep buying them and buying them even if I don't need them. I once had so many flat screens in my house, my wife was ready to tell me, she's like, what are you doing with all these flat screens? Everyone was a flat screen. I just saw it was the coolest technology. And they always have this planned obsolescence where every year they come up with the next type of flat screen. Now it's 8K. It'll be 16K. Freaking nose. Point is, I can't even see that small and they keep coming out of flat screens and I keep buying them. But here's the deal. The actual profit that a company, whether it's a Fry's or a Best Buy or a Hobby No matter how many, one of the hundreds of thousands of operators, the amount they make on those types of profits, the margins are relatively slim. Much more lucrative is when they say to you, hey, how would you like the three year warranty that comes along with this? So after anything happens, you know, we stand behind it, no problem. We'll fix it. We'll exchange it, whatnot for three years. Or you could take the two year, even the one year warranty. And typically that will be on top of the manufacturers warranty. So while the manufacturers warranty might be up for maybe a year, they'll take theirs for three years. It'll be more inclusive. Now, I'm not sure what the exact margins are for any particular store because they vary, but they're staggeringly large. These warranties, they're very, very profitable for the stores that issue them. Because at the end of the day, most people don't cash in their warranties. It's almost like the insurance business. You're getting money upfront for a payout down the road. So you get to even work with that money. So there's money made on interest and so forth, very lucrative industry. But here's what kills me. After shopping in Fry's, I went back about six or seven times in the three months and I kept testing the salesman there and saying, so tell me about this warranty after it's like, oh, yeah, it's a three year warranty. Do you want it? I was like, no, thanks. Like, okay, go back in. After it was like, oh, by the way, would you like the three year warranty? No, you want to yet? No. I said, okay, have a nice day. They said, do you understand the insanity of that type of behavior in a sales person? Now let's flash forward to a different company. I'm not going to say the name. I did a train for one of the largest electronics distributors in the world. I went into this company and I was able to increase their sales by 32% in this division. That's massive for an industry. But guess what? That was the least of what I did for them. The main value proposition was, I showed them how to take their warranty compliance rate from 10 or 12% that it was to 85%. I showed their sales person and I get 85% of the customers to take the warranty and that had the company's profits sore. What was I doing that was so special? Very simple. I was treating the warranty sale like one should treat it as its own independent sale. Understand this, when you ask for the order and the person says, yeah, let's do a great and now you have the after sales, whether it's a warranty, rustproofing, undercoding, specialized, that represents its own individual sale where the three 10s have to line up. You have an introduction, a body, a close. You have intelligence, you have obviously, if you ran proper straight line patterns, you would have probably enough intelligence. You might want to ask a couple of questions, by the way, but you would have your transition of, well, let me say this to you, by the way, based on what you're saying, there actually is another product I strongly recommend that you look at. It's the three year warranty. Let me tell you why I think this is the best deal we offer. I would have a full blown, beautiful pitch prepared with the clothes and looping patterns. That's what I did for this company and they went soaring through the roof in terms of probability. Let's say you got a three year warranty. They say, nah, that's your, well, let me show you. The idea of protection makes sense to you. They're like, well, yeah, let me do this. I'm not going to do the whole pattern. I'm going to lay it out for you in the exercise there and the exercise you had to do, right? But you step them down from a three year to a two year to a one year warranty. The perfect step down sale. It's a no brainer. The deal is, is that after sales must be treated like their own independent sales. All that you have is you have some benefit in order to you on that second 10 and the third 10. On the first 10, you're starting off wherever you must start off, meaning with their preconceived notions. And guess what? When it comes to warranties, what strikes your mind right now? Chances are like, oh yeah, I know. Let's just help the company out. Those many people go into a sale thinking that when they hear about the warranty out here, that's just something the company uses to make extra money. They don't want it. So what would I be doing? Realizing that I will be ceding the response that into my actual presentation. You know, I'd start saying, listen, you know, most cases people offer warranties. They don't really make a big difference. This one is different. Let me tell you exactly why. And I position my warranty as a must buy, not lying, using tonality, body language, and the words that have been strategically prepared to make the best possible case, essentially what the truth well told. And I've asked for the order in the same way I asked for the order on the main product. I promise you this, if you put the same amount of effort into structuring your after sale, as you did your primary sale, by default, your closer it will double for after sales. Think about that. You can't close a deal. Oh, by the way, would you like the warranty? And that's your clothes. Do you understand that companies make a bloody fortune on warranties? And by the way, I'm not saying at the expense of clients. See, a company making a fortune on a product and a client not getting a deal, that's not mutually exclusive. You could have a very lucrative, probably, listen, insurance, Warren Buffett, he owns massive insurance companies, right? Is he robbing from no? He gives amazing value. It's just insurance is a very lucrative field. Well, that's what essentially warranties are. I don't even want to really focus just on warranties, because this is about in general all after sales. The point is, they are their own independent sales, requiring full straight line pitches, attention to detail, strategic preparation. You ask for the order, looping patterns, step-by-sales, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, and there you go. You do that. Watch what happens. All right. Now, let's go straight to a quiz here.
